narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students. However, the admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 49% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—right on the edge, but enough to tip the decision toward rejection to minimize the school's risk of low bar passage rates. This prediction hinged on just a few key aspects of your profile, with your LSAT score emerging as the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your LSAT of 23 was notably lower than the typical applicant's 37, signaling to the model a weaker foundation in the analytical skills crucial for bar success. Nearly balancing that out was your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, which is close to the average of 3.21 and strongly pulled the prediction in the opposite direction, suggesting you have the academic grit to pass. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of about 5.5—nudged the odds further toward failure, as it reflects performance lagging behind many peers at a similar stage. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your combination of a low LSAT and weaker later-year grades created a riskier profile, even with your decent GPA offering some reassurance. The model doesn't weigh things like your race or gender directly, but it focuses on these proven predictors from real student outcomes. While this rejection stings, it's not a final verdict on your potential—many applicants in similar spots have boosted their chances by retaking the LSAT or strengthening their GPA through post-bac courses. Your next steps could include targeted LSAT prep to aim for 35 or higher, seeking academic advising to improve decile rankings in any ongoing studies, and reapplying after building a clearer track record of upward momentum. With focused effort on those core areas, you can shift the model's view and open doors at schools that value your full-time commitment and background. Keep pushing; your story is still unfolding.
